summary: The three central metabolic hubs are glycolysis, the TCA cycle, and the electron transport chain.
The three central metabolic hubs are beta-oxidation, the urea cycle, and the pentose phosphate pathway.
The three central metabolic hubs are gluconeogenesis, glycogenesis, and lipogenesis.
Glucagon promotes fuel mobilisation during fasting, while insulin promotes storage in the fed state.
Insulin promotes fuel mobilisation during fasting, while glucagon promotes storage in the fed state.
Both insulin and glucagon promote storage in both fed and fasting states.
Cyanide poisoning blocks Complex IV of the electron transport chain, preventing oxygen from acting as the terminal electron acceptor.
Cyanide poisoning blocks Complex I of the electron transport chain, preventing NADH oxidation.
Cyanide poisoning blocks ATP synthase directly, preventing ATP production.
The chemiosmotic theory by Peter Mitchell explains how the proton gradient drives ATP synthesis by ATP synthase.
The chemiosmotic theory by Hans Krebs explains how substrate-level phosphorylation drives ATP synthesis.
The chemiosmotic theory explains how calcium gradients drive ATP synthesis.
Complete aerobic oxidation of one glucose molecule yields approximately 36-38 ATP molecules.
Complete aerobic oxidation of one glucose molecule yields approximately 4 ATP molecules.
Complete aerobic oxidation of one glucose molecule yields approximately 100 ATP molecules.
Uncoupling proteins in brown adipose tissue dissipate the proton gradient as heat instead of ATP production.
Uncoupling proteins in white adipose tissue increase ATP production by enhancing the proton gradient.
Uncoupling proteins are found only in skeletal muscle and increase mechanical efficiency.
